text: We started with a right BATS approach and encountered adhesions.
These adhesions were taken down with hook diathermy after assessing the sympathetic chain.
The patient had a history of previous right-sided pneumothorax, and we anticipated that this
problem arising.
As the patient was on anti-platelet therapy it was key to do this carefully to avoid excessive bleeding.
Here we see that we had to carefully
remove adhesions from the upper lip of the lung to the mediastinal pleura.
We were then able to assess the sympathetic chain.
A hook was used to assess the exact position at where we would make our incision using the hook diaphragm.
Careful, sharp dissection is performed to open up the mediastinal pleura.
Endoscopic suction is employed along with CO2 insufflation to ensure a clear operative field.
The patient has general anaesthetic double lumen tube intubation
to enable lung isolation.
Here we can see that as the patient is on antiplatelet therapy, bleeding encountered must be dealt with carefully.
The patient has a large body habitus and excess mediastinal fat and fluid.
Once the sympathetic chain is carefully dissected out and hemostasis is achieved, it is then possible to divide the sympathetic chain.
The patient is counselled for the risk of post-operative Horner syndrome.
Again, it is very important to carefully assess the position of the fourth rib to ensure that the division of the lower half of the stellar ganglia is performed correctly.
This approach uses a two-port technique.
Here we can now see that we have approached the left side now.
Chest tube drainage was inserted for the right dance approach with the bleeding and adhesions.
A probe peanut from the contralateral side is pushed as we can see here, so we can establish the position of the sympathetic chain on the left side.
Surgical assistance
We're standing on the opposite side of the patient who is in the supine position, pushing as we can see here, so that we can identify exactly our landmarks.
Again, a hook is used to be careful as to exactly where our first incision is made to open up the medial spinal cord on the left side.
Again, a hooked diathermy is used to open up the medial style of pura and dissect out the sympathetic chain on the left side.
In this specific case, there were no adhesions and therefore this side was performed more routinely as described in the literature.
Again, endoscopic suction is used at the end of the procedure to suck out any remaining blood clots and actually add up the hemostasis, particularly as no chest tube was left on this side.
